title: Fix SVG attribute serialization in remix/component to preserve filter semantics
body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an SVG serialization issue in `remix/component` where certain SVG attributes were being normalized in ways that changed browser behavior, especially for filter effects. In some cases, attributes that need canonical SVG casing were emitted in a different form, causing filter coordinate systems and related rendering behavior to diverge from React/browser expectations.

The fix hardens SVG attribute normalization so output is consistent between SSR and client updates and aligns with canonical SVG behavior. This should resolve rendering issues like disappearing/mispositioned filter effects while reducing risk of similar SVG attribute regressions going forward.

## The problem

A filter authored like this:

```tsx
<svg>
  <defs>
    <filter
      id="timeline-shadow"
      filterUnits="userSpaceOnUse"
      x="0"
      y="0"
      width="1200"
      height="200"
    >
      <feGaussianBlur stdDeviation="2.5" />
    </filter>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

would be serialized with transformed attribute names like:

```tsx
<filter
  id="timeline-shadow"
  filter-units="userSpaceOnUse"
  x="0"
  y="0"
  width="1200"
  height="200"
>
  <feGaussianBlur std-deviation="2.5"></feGaussianBlur>
</filter>
```

For attributes such as `filterUnits`, browsers treat this differently than canonical `filterUnits`, which can lead to incorrect filter coordinate behavior and visual regressions (e.g. effects disappearing or rendering in the wrong bounds

## Root cause

`remix/component` used a generic SVG camelCase→kebab-case fallback with a small exception list. For example, `filterUnits` was not exempted, so output became `filter-units`, which browsers treat as a different attribute and therefore do not apply to SVG unit semantics.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm --filter @remix-run/component test -- --run src/test/vdom.svg.test.tsx src/test/stream.test.tsx src/test/hydration.attributes.test.tsx`
- [x] `pnpm run lint`
- [x] Manual browser probe confirmed canonical camelCase `*Units` attrs affect SVG animated enum semantics, while dashed forms do not for affected attrs.
author: brookslybrand

commits.message:
fix(component): preserve canonical SVG unit attributes in SSR and DOM updates

Remix Component was serializing semantic SVG unit attributes like filterUnits as kebab-case (filter-units), which causes browsers to ignore intended filter/gradient coordinate systems. Add shared SVG attribute normalization across stream and diff-props plus focused regression coverage for SSR, client runtime, and hydration.
expand SVG attribute normalization and improve test coverage
fix(component): correctly remove namespaced svg attributes

Hello Remix team 👋

Is this intentional?

## feat(data-table): support cross-schema column resolves

### ✨ Summary

This PR adds support for resolving data-table columns across schemas.

The main goal is to make column resolution more flexible when data comes from multiple schema sources, while keeping the existing behavior unchanged for single-schema scenarios.

### 🧠 Motivation

Some data-table use cases require columns that reference values defined in a different schema than the primary one.  
Before this change, column resolution assumed a single schema context, which made cross-schema scenarios harder or required workarounds.

This update introduces cross-schema resolution so that:

- columns can resolve data from related schemas
- configuration remains explicit and predictable
- existing tables continue to work without changes

### 🔧 What changed

- Add cross-schema column resolve logic
- Update resolver flow to support schema-aware lookup
- Adjust internal typing/handling to ensure compatibility
- Keep backward compatibility with current behavior

### 🧪 Validation

- Existing data-table behavior remains unchanged
- Cross-schema cases resolve correctly
- No breaking API changes

After
```
Platform: Darwin (25.2.0)
CPU: Apple M4 Pro
Date: 2/21/2026, 10:44:01 AM
Node.js v24.6.0
┌──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────────┬────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐
│ (index)          │ 1 small file     │ 1 large file     │ 100 small files  │ 5 large files     │ 1 large file (adversarial) │ 5 large files (adversarial) │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────┼──────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
│ multipart-parser │ '0.01 ms ± 0.02' │ '0.73 ms ± 0.04' │ '0.03 ms ± 0.01' │ '7.47 ms ± 0.26'  │ '0.19 ms ± 0.05'           │ '2.12 ms ± 0.44'            │
│ multipasta       │ '0.01 ms ± 0.04' │ '0.72 ms ± 0.03' │ '0.11 ms ± 0.02' │ '7.45 ms ± 0.16'  │ '0.71 ms ± 0.32'           │ '7.18 ms ± 0.51'            │
│ busboy           │ '0.05 ms ± 0.16' │ '2.28 ms ± 0.16' │ '0.17 ms ± 0.04' │ '22.35 ms ± 0.39' │ '0.46 ms ± 0.02'           │ '4.56 ms ± 0.14'            │
│ @fastify/busboy  │ '0.04 ms ± 0.13' │ '0.84 ms ± 0.08' │ '0.32 ms ± 0.18' │ '8.42 ms ± 0.16'  │ '0.29 ms ± 0.03'           │ '2.99 ms ± 0.10'            │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────────────┴────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘
```

## Summary

This PR improves `@remix-run/multipart-parser` performance work in three steps, following a measure-first workflow:

- **Added a stronger benchmark methodology** so we can trust perf signals before changing parser internals:
  - benchmark targeting (`parser`, `benchmark`, `iterations`)
  - new runner options: `--steady`, `--metrics`, `--warmup=<count>`
  - throughput + heap delta reporting
  - adversarial multipart fixtures to stress boundary scanning behavior
- **Implemented a carry-boundary parsing optimization** in `MultipartParser.write()`:
  - avoid unconditional `carry + chunk` buffer copying in body state
  - scan boundary candidates across virtual two-segment input
  - preserve existing behavior in non-body states
- **Added web-model regression coverage** for streamed boundary-edge cases:
  - boundary split across chunks
  - partial boundary tail at chunk edge
  - boundary starting exactly at next chunk edge

## Why this approach

Early micro-optimizations were mostly noisy/inconclusive. The new benchmark harness made it clear the main weakness was cross-chunk boundary handling, especially under adversarial patterns. That guided a focused parser change rather than more local micro-tweaks.

## Performance outcome

Using the new steady-state benchmarks:

- `1-large-file` / `5-large-files`: stays competitive with `multipasta` (generally tie/near-tie).
- 100 small files and adversarial cases saw a major improvement for `multipart-parser`, with repeated runs showing large gains (not single-run noise), e.g.:
  - `5-large-files-adversarial`: roughly `~7.6ms` down to `~2.2ms` for `multipart-parser` in this benchmark setup.

## Test coverage added

New regression tests in the **web `Request` path** (`multipart-request.test.ts`) ensure carry/boundary edge handling remains correct for streamed input chunk boundaries.

## Test plan

title: Frames: out-of-order streaming server rendering, selective hydration, composable async UI and granular refresh
body: Here is the flow from 1. streamed server rendering 2. frames 3. hydration 4. ui updates from frame reload (hybrid dom/vdom diff) ## 1. Streamed Server Rendering Everything starts with `renderToStream(node, options)` in `packages/component/src/lib/stream.ts`. It takes a component tree and returns a `ReadableStream`. ### Segment tree building The renderer walks the JSX tree and builds an intermediate `Segment` tree rather than emitting HTML directly. There are three segment types: - `static` — a raw HTML string - `composite` — an array of child segments - `frame` — a frame boundary with potentially async content ```ts let root = buildSegment(node, context, '') await resolveBlocking(root) let html = serializeSegment(root) let finalHtml = finalizeHtml(html, context) let bytes = encoder.encode(finalHtml) controller.enqueue(bytes) ``` ### Blocking vs non-blocking frames When `buildSegment` encounters a `` component, it calls `resolveFrame(src)` to fetch the frame's content: - **Blocking** (no `fallback` prop): Must resolve before any HTML is sent. Content is inlined in the initial chunk. - **Non-blocking** (has `fallback` prop): Fallback HTML is inlined immediately. Real content streams later as `

title: Switch `nightly` builds to continuous builds of `main` in a `preview` branch
body:
Ryan pointed out a small gap in our workflow - while a PR is open you can install a working build via the PR `preview/####` branch, but once it's merged the `preview/####` branch is deleted and you need to wait for the `nightly` build before you can install that work again.  This is problematic for being able to merge and immediately test/demo new work.

Because we're not publishing to npm, there's no reason to only build `main` every night, we can just as easily do it on every new commit.  

This PR changes the nightly flow to instead build to a `preview` branch on every new commit to `main` so we don't have these gaps waiting for a nightly build.  Then you can run this anytime to test the latest `main`:

```
pnpm install "remix-run/remix#preview&path:packages/remix"
```

title: route-pattern: rank matches
body: supersedes #10956
author: pcattori

title: route-pattern: rank
body:
# RoutePattern Ranking

## Rank Structure

```ts
type Rank = {
  hostname: ParamRange[]
  pathname: ParamRange[]
  search: Search.Constraints
}

type ParamRange = {
  type: ':' | '*'
  begin: number
  end: number
}
```

## Constraints

- `ParamRange[]` is ordered by `begin` (equivalently by `end`)
- Ranges never overlap
- Indices are relative to the part (hostname or pathname)
  - hostname: `0` to `url.hostname.length`
  - pathname: `0` to `url.pathname.length`
- Protocol and port are ignored (static only)

## Example

Pattern: `https://:subdomain.example.com/posts/*rest/:id`

Matched URL: `https://api.example.com/posts/2024/archive/123`

```ts
const rank = {
  hostname: [
    { type: ':', begin: 0, end: 3 }  // "api"
  ],
  pathname: [
    { type: '*', begin: 6, end: 19 }, // "2024/archive"
    { type: ':', begin: 20, end: 23 } // "123"
  ],
  search: new Map() // from pattern.ast.search
}
```

## Comparison

Compare in order: hostname → pathname → search → insertion order

### Hostname Comparison

1. Create `Int8Array` of length `url.hostname.length` (initialized to `0`)
2. For each `ParamRange`, fill indices `[begin, end)` with:
   - `0` = static (default)
   - `1` = `:` (variable param)
   - `2` = `*` (wildcard)
3. Compare segments **right-to-left** (hostname specificity is reversed)
   - Find `.` separators, start after the last `.`
   - Within each segment, compare char-by-char **left-to-right**
   - Lower value wins (static > variable > wildcard)
   - If segment tied, move to next segment (right-to-left)

### Pathname Comparison

Optimized char-by-char comparison without allocating Int8Array:

1. Track `i = 0` (current char index in URL pathname)
2. Track current range index for each pattern (index into `ParamRange[]`)
3. Skip `i` to `min(currentRange1.begin, currentRange2.begin)`
4. At `i`:
   - If one range starts after `i` → other has static content → static wins
   - Otherwise compare range types: `:` > `*`
   - If same type → increment `i`
5. When `i` reaches range `end`, increment that pattern's current range index
6. Repeat until winner determined or all ranges exhausted

### Search Comparison

More constrained search wins:
- `?q=1` > `?q=` > `?q`
- `?q&a` vs `?q&b` → tie

Convert `Search.Constraints` to counts:
```ts
{
  exactValue: number  // e.g. ?q=1
  anyValue: number    // e.g. ?q=
  key: number         // e.g. ?q
}
```

Compare in order (higher wins):
1. `exactValue` count
2. `anyValue` count (tiebreaker)
3. `key` count (tiebreaker)

### Insertion Order

Final tiebreaker: lower insertion order wins (earlier = higher priority)

## Performance

- **Rank computation**: Must be fast (computed during matching, possibly incrementally in TrieMatcher)
- **Rank comparison**: Can be slower (only compare actual matches, often just one)

title: chore: support publishing prerelease `remix` package
body:
We need to publish `remix` as an alpha while keeping all other packages on `latest`. Unfortunately `pnpm publish --recursive` doesn't support mixed dist-tags, so when remix is in prerelease mode, we now publish in two phases:
- First publish all packages other than `remix` first, as `latest`
- Then publish `remix` by itself (using pnpm's `--filter`) with its prerelease tag (e.g. `alpha`)

This PR also adds `prerelease.json` to the `remix` package with `{ "tag": "alpha" }` and a major change file for the initial alpha release. Prerelease mode is only supported for the `remix` package since it's the only one that needs to publish alongside an existing stable version on npm. All of this has been documented in AGENTS.md and CONTRIBUTING.md.
- From now on, normal `major.`/`minor.`/`patch.` change files for `remix` bump the prerelease counter, e.g. `3.0.0-alpha.0` -> `3.0.0-alpha.1`.
- Changing the tag in `prerelease.json` will alter the tag and reset the counter, e.g. `3.0.0-alpha.7` -> `3.0.0-beta.0`.
- To graduate to stable, delete `prerelease.json` and add a major change file to bump to a stable version, e.g. `3.0.0-rc.7` -> `3.0.0`.

You can also now preview the publish flow with `node ./scripts/publish.ts --dry-run` to see which publish command(s) will be run.

title: route-pattern: PartPattern.{parse,variants,toString}
body: ## Context During the development of `route-pattern`, the design for the Route Pattern DSL changed many times. This wasn't unexpected, but it did mean that the matching APIs (`ArrayMatcher`, `TrieMatcher`, etc.) and parsing algorithms always had to "catch up" to the newest DSL changes. Over time, this led to some bolted-on bits and some gaps in the matching/parsing. Additionally, the current `ArrayMatcher` and `TrieMatcher` aren't guaranteed to return the same "best" match. `ArrayMatcher` just bails as soon as it finds its first match and `TrieMatcher` does some ad-hoc scoring. The goal moving forward is to spec out the parsing/matching (via unit tests + docs) and implement a simple, consistent ranking metric for `ArrayMatcher`, `TrieMatcher`, or any other matcher. Here's my plan: - `PartPattern.{parse,variants,toString}` (this PR) - `RoutePattern.{parse,join}` - `TrieMatcher.{add,matchAll}` (w/o ranking) - Ranking + `TrieMatcher.{match, matchAll}` - `ArrayMatcher.{add,match,matchAll}` ## `PartPattern` As a first step towards this goal, this PR implements a new design for the parts of a Route Pattern that are allowed to use the pattern DSL. Today, that includes the protocol, hostname, and pathname, but in the future its likely that the protocol will be simplified to just accept the known network schemes (`http`, `https`, `http(s)`, `ws`, `wss`, `ws(s)`, `ftp`, ...). Knowing that we want to eventually use `PartPattern`s within `RoutePattern`s to power a `TrieMatcher`, this new implementation aims for a simpler flat AST: This AST representation has a couple benefits over the previous implementation: **Traversal** is simpler and faster since its just a for-loop where you can decide to skip any optionals as you go. Specifically, **variants** (for construction trie branches) are trivial to implement. **Feature detection** is simpler and faster. For example, does this part pattern have any params? Check `ast.paramNames.length > 0`? Any optionals? Check `ast.optionals.size > 0`. Etc. **Joining patterns** is more efficient. Previously, `RoutePattern.join` reparsed the entire pathname on every invocation whereas with this AST, we can simple concatenate the two `pathnames` (adding a `/` text node between them as necessary) and quickly apply an offset to the second pattern's `paramNames` and `optionals`. To be clear, the goal isn't necessary to be faster at parsing part patterns — that can come later with optimizations if needed — but to have a purpose-built structure for our DSL.

title: fix(node-fetch-server): use `:authority` header to set URL of http/2 requests
body:
Hello :wave:,

A react-router 7 app that was switched to use SSL for local development introduced a bug where the host of the `request` URL was always `localhost` with no port. I believe it is the same root cause as https://github.com/remix-run/react-router/issues/14010 however the behavior in the latest release differs from that of the description in that issue.

The problem appears to be that this library only considers the `Host` header. [RFC 7540](https://httpwg.org/specs/rfc7540.html#HttpRequest) states:
> Clients that generate HTTP/2 requests directly SHOULD use the :authority pseudo-header field instead of the Host header field. 

This issue can be demonstrated with the http2 demo included in this repo. When it is updated to output the URL like so:
```
diff --git a/packages/node-fetch-server/demos/http2/server.js b/packages/node-fetch-server/demos/http2/server.js
index d5847aa72..c88ff3f5a 100644
--- a/packages/node-fetch-server/demos/http2/server.js
+++ b/packages/node-fetch-server/demos/http2/server.js
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ server.on(
     let url = new URL(request.url)

     if (url.pathname === '/') {
-      return new Response('Hello HTTP/2!', {
+      return new Response(`Hello HTTP/2! ${url}\n`, {
         headers: {
           'Content-Type': 'text/plain',
         },
```

On the main branch this is the output:
```
$ curl --cacert server.crt https://localhost:44100
Hello HTTP/2! https://localhost/
```

With this PR this is the output:
```
$ curl --cacert server.crt https://localhost:44100
Hello HTTP/2! https://localhost:44100/

```
author: phiggins

commits.message: Use `:authority` header to set URL of http/2 requests.

route-pattern: fix type distribution for `Parse`

At first, `Parse` used `T extends any ? ... : ...` to force TS to
perform union distribution. Later, that was changed to `[T] extends [string] ? ... : ...`

extends string>` in the generic declaration.
As a result, `Parse` no longer performed union distribution which caused
it to incorrectly compute types when unions were passed in.
Additionally, the benchmarks for `Parse` were incorrect since they only
reflected the type instantiations when incorrectly computing parsed
types.

To fix this, a test was added that explicitly passes in a union type to
`Parse` and the corresponding type benchmarks were updated. The new test
unsurprisingly fails with `[T] extends [string] ? ...: ...` and passed
with `T extends any ? ... : ...`.

title: Fix Windows path handling with fileURLToPath
body:
## Summary

Fixes #10868

This PR fixes test failures on Windows caused by incorrect path handling when using `new URL(import.meta.url).pathname`.

## Problem

On Windows, `new URL(import.meta.url).pathname` returns paths like `/D:/workspace/...` (with a leading slash). When combined with `path.dirname()` or `path.resolve()`, this results in duplicated drive letters (`D:\D:\workspace\...`), causing file not found errors.

## Changes

### Path Handling

Replaced `new URL(import.meta.url).pathname` with `fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)` in:
- `packages/tar-parser/test/utils.ts`
- `scripts/utils/packages.js`
- `packages/node-fetch-server/demos/http2/server.js`
- `packages/static-middleware/demos/list-files/server.js`

```diff
- const __dirname = path.dirname(new URL(import.meta.url).pathname)
+ import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'
+ const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))
```

### Line Ending Normalization

Added line ending normalization in `packages/mime/scripts/codegen.test.ts` to handle CRLF/LF differences between Windows and Unix systems:

```typescript
let normalizeLineEndings = (str: string) => str.replace(/\r\n/g, '\n')
```

title: feat(fetch-router): add alias functions for standard HTTP verb paths
body:
Drafted an idea I had watching the reveal livestream. This PR adds functional aliases for simple HTTP routes.

The route objects returned are super simple here, but providing them as functions to call would make the framework appear more consistent when defining a route using helpers. For instance, this would be an existing way to express some routes:

```ts
let routes = createRoutes({
  home:  { method: 'GET', pattern: '/' },
  books: resources('books'),
  profile: resource('profile'),
})
```

After this change, we would be able to use a consistent syntax for resources, formActions, and single-method routes.

```ts
let routes = createRoutes({
  home: get('/'),
  books: resources('books'),
  profile: resource('profile'),
})
```

I followed the `resource.ts` file to try to get the style right, please do advise on anything I could update!

title: Add session package
body:
This PR adds a new `@remix-run/session` package and a `session` middleware to `fetch-router`.

It's a complete rewrite of the session handling code we currently have in Remix v2 and RR v7. The main motivations for this work were:

- Less ceremony around session storage creation
- More powerful session management API that supports regenerating IDs and smarter/automatic handling of `session.flash()` values
- Add `FileSessionStorage` for storing sessions on the filesystem

This PR closes #10790 and #10794, and supersedes #10811

title: fix(fetch-router): make `Router`'s `fetch` spec-compliant
body:
As @rossipedia [mentioned on Discord](https://discord.com/channels/770287896669978684/940264701785423992/1426664672878268591), `Router`'s `fetch` currently isn't spec-compliant if you pass both `Request` & `init`

> A [`RequestInit`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/RequestInit) object containing any custom settings that you want to apply to the request.
> 
> If you construct a new `Request` from an existing `Request`, any options you set in an _options_ argument for the new request replace any corresponding options set in the original `Request`.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Request/Request#options
author: MichaelDeBoey

reviews.body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes the `Router`'s `fetch` method to be spec-compliant when both a `Request` object and `RequestInit` options are provided. Previously, the method incorrectly ignored the `init` parameter when a `Request` was passed, but the fetch specification requires that options in `init` should replace corresponding options in the original `Request`. - Simplified the request construction logic to always use `new Request(input, init)` - Added test coverage to verify that `RequestInit` options properly override `Request` properties ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | packages/fetch-router/src/lib/router.ts | Updated fetch method to always construct new Request with both input and init parameters | | packages/fetch-router/src/lib/router.test.ts | Added test case to verify RequestInit options override Request properties | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.

title: Updates for v2 releases
body:
Updates v2 scripts to align with the same publishing process used in `main` by v3.  It'll be a 2 step process:

- Tag the version in the v2 branch via `pnpm run version 2.x.y`
- Push to github which will publish from CI via `pnpm run publish`

title: Add github workflow for v2 publishes
body:
This is a copy of the [`publish.yaml`](https://github.com/remix-run/remix/blob/main/.github/workflows/publish.yaml) workflow but instead of ignoring `remix@x.y.z` tags it will only kick off for those tags, and thus will only be triggered for releases from the v2 branch.

Sibling PR to update the v2 branch to follow the same github release flow where we tag locally and push to github for publishing from CI: https://github.com/remix-run/remix/pull/10748

commits.message: Add low-level createRequest and sendResponse APIs
Fix request abort signal firing too early
Add version script to all packages
Add new test package for version/publish scripts
No need to strip scope from package name

Upgrade to TypeScript 5.7.2

Also, use node's built-in type stripping instead of @swc-node/register.

Also, add support for node:test runner VS Code plugin.
Add some links to doc comments
Add workspaces key to root package.json

feat: use object instead of array for sending headers

Update `sendResponse` to use an object to add support for libraries such as express while maintaining `node:http` and `node:https` compatibility.

Merge pull request #37 from mjackson/response_headers

feat: use object instead of array for sending headers
Add Content-Encoding, Content-Language, and Location support
Small perf optimization for string getters
Use constants for internal key names
Use local tsc for running tasks in VSCode
Handle zero-length files in tar parsing

local-file-storage: scalability + race fixes

This commit fixes race conditions and potential performance issues

## Race conditions:

- Multiple calls to `set` caused race conditions with the central metafile index.
- Not only did this cause the set to fail, but it often corrupted the metafile causing a permanent, unrecoverable error since the metafile could no longer be parsed.

## Scalability:

- The single metadata file would eventually grow large in a successful application and become both a memory issue and performance bottleneck
- Additionally, storing all files in a single directory would eventually slow down the file system’s access of files in the directory.

## Changes:

- Removes race conditions with atomic reads/writes
  - Removes the metafile and instead relies on SHA-256 hashes for file keys
  - Stores metadata alongside each file as .meta.json instead of central index
  - removes filename collision handling since it’s unnecessary with hash-based names
- Performance
  - Shards files into subdirectories using first 8 chars of SHA-256 hash to maintain file system performance
  - Without a metafile, it is no longer a memory or performance bottleneck

Should rebase/squash the commits before merging, added the failing test first to prove the bug.
Use web crypto API instead of node:crypto

title: docs(utils/sessions): improve docs
body:
See https://github.com/remix-run/remix/pull/7225#issuecomment-1688920918 for details. In the absence of a larger rewrite I think this should substantially improve clarity and reduce confusion.

Key bits include:

- Clarifying that regardless of your session storage strategy, if you don't `commitSession()`, you're losing your changes.
- Further clarifying that if you are using cookie session storage and you don't `Set-Cookie`, you're still losing your changes.
- Correcting the cookie size limit. It's actually 1024 bytes for a single cookie value in Chromium and Firefox (sources: [RFC 6265](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-httpbis-rfc6265bis), [Chrome Platform Status](https://chromestatus.com/feature/4946713618939904)). 4 KB is the limit for all cookie names + values combined.
- Consistently recommending `app/session.server.ts` over `app/session.ts`.

The one more opinionated thing I did as well was to remove the lengthy example under `session.flash()`, because there is already an example of the same thing earlier in the doc.

Please let me know if there's anything else I can do to help ship this, so that I can stop making the same mistakes over and over every time I read this doc page. Thanks!

@brophdawg11
author: aaronadamsCA

commits.message: Improve clarity and consistency of Sessions docs

title: fix(remix-serve): remove redundant `@remix-run/node/install` import
body:
`remix-serve` cli seems calling `installGlobals` function of `@remix-run/node` package twice. In my usage, it's called with 2 different `nativeFetch` flags and that seems causing unnecessary dependency (`@remix-run/web-fetch`) loaded on the server runtime.

This PR removes the first call of `installGlobals` (by removing import of `@remix-run/node/install`), and tries to avoid the above situation.

title: docs(start/tutorial): remove unnecessary `useEffect`
body:
Using useEffect for syncing state with props (or other external state) is unnecessary, and can trigger additional renders and potentially flashes of incorrect content. We can use a useState to detect changes in order to trigger a sync. 

This is explicitly allowed by React – https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

While an in-depth tutorial of React is out of scope for Remix's tutorial, it remains the case that people look to projects like Remix for good examples – we ought to avoid effects where they aren't necessary (and are explicitly discouraged).
author: nathggns

commits.message:
Remove unnecessary useEffect from tutorial code example

Using useEffect for syncing state with props (or other external state) is unnecessary, and can trigger additional renders and potentially flashes of incorrect content. We can use a useState to detect changes in order to trigger a sync. 

This is explicitly allowed by React – https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes
